name: DC Motor
fullName: DC Gear Motor with L298N / L293D Motor Driver
tagline: Continuous rotation motor controlled via a motor driver for speed and direction control
description: A DC motor converts electrical energy into rotational motion. Unlike servo motors, DC motors spin continuously and require a motor driver IC (L298N or L293D) to control direction and speed from an Arduino — Arduino pins cannot source enough current to drive a motor directly. Speed is controlled via PWM, and direction by setting the driver's IN pins HIGH or LOW. Used in robots, fans, conveyor belts, and RC vehicles.

specs.controlSignals: PWM for speed + 2 digital pins for direction per motor

pinout.name: Motor Terminals
pinout.desc: Connect DC motor leads to OUT1 and OUT2 on motor driver

pinout.name: Enable A (PWM)
pinout.desc: PWM pin to control motor A speed (connect to Arduino PWM pin)

pinout.name: Direction 1
pinout.desc: HIGH = forward when IN2 is LOW

pinout.name: Direction 2
pinout.desc: HIGH = reverse when IN1 is LOW

pinout.name: Motor Power
pinout.desc: External motor power supply (up to 12V for L298N)

pinout.name: GND
pinout.desc: Shared ground — connect to Arduino GND and power supply GND

pinout.name: 5V (L298N)
pinout.desc: 5V output from L298N to power Arduino (when using ≥7V motor supply)

wiringNote: Always use a motor driver — never connect a motor directly to an Arduino pin. Diodes or a driver with built-in flyback protection prevent voltage spikes from damaging the microcontroller.
commonMistakes: Connecting motor directly to Arduino pin → draws too much current, burns the pin or MCU
Not sharing GND between Arduino and motor driver → motor doesn't respond
Forgetting to enable ENA/ENB pin → motor stays off regardless of IN pins
Running motor supply through Arduino → voltage spike on brake/stop damages Arduino
